name: Perito Moreno Glacier
about: Perito Moreno is one of the most famous glaciers in Patagonia and one of the most visited natural landmarks in Argentina. It is known for its massive blue ice wall, dramatic calving events, and its position inside Los Glaciares National Park. Large blocks of ice regularly break from the glacier front and crash into Lago Argentino, creating one of the most powerful glacier spectacles in the world.
keyFacts: One of the few glaciers historically known for periods of stability and advance
Part of the Southern Patagonian Ice Field
Located inside Los Glaciares National Park
Famous for dramatic ice ruptures and thunderous calving events
Its front reaches Lago Argentino
Popular for viewing platforms and guided glacier experiences

name: Vatnajökull Glacier
about: Vatnajökull is Iceland’s largest ice cap and one of the most impressive glacier systems in Europe. It covers volcanoes, mountains, valleys, and hidden subglacial landscapes. Its outlet glaciers flow in many directions, creating ice caves, glacier lagoons, black sand ice beaches, and dramatic frozen scenery that changes with each season.
keyFacts: Largest ice cap in Iceland
Covers around 8% of Iceland’s land area
Contains many outlet glaciers
Lies above active volcanic systems
Famous for blue ice caves and glacier lagoons
Part of Vatnajökull National Park
One of Europe’s largest glacier masses

name: Aletsch Glacier
about: Aletsch Glacier is the largest glacier in the Alps and one of the most recognizable ice formations in Europe. It flows through the high mountain landscape of the Bernese Alps, forming a long frozen river between steep peaks. Its scale, curved flow lines, and surrounding alpine scenery make it an important symbol of glacier change in the European mountains.
keyFacts: Largest glacier in the Alps
Located in the Jungfrau-Aletsch region
Recognized as part of a UNESCO World Heritage landscape
Fed by several high-altitude accumulation zones
Known for long visible medial moraines
Important for studying alpine glacier retreat

name: Lambert Glacier
about: Lambert Glacier is one of the largest glacier systems on Earth. It drains a huge part of the East Antarctic Ice Sheet and flows toward the Amery Ice Shelf. Unlike tourist-accessible mountain glaciers, Lambert is a vast polar ice system, important for understanding Antarctic ice movement, sea-level research, and the hidden structure of the frozen continent.
keyFacts: One of the world’s largest glacier systems
Located in East Antarctica
Drains ice toward the Amery Ice Shelf
Part of a massive Antarctic drainage basin
Extremely remote and difficult to access
Important for satellite observation and climate research
Represents a large part of Antarctic ice flow dynamics

name: Franz Josef Glacier
location: Westland Tai Poutini National Park, New Zealand
about: Franz Josef Glacier is a steep and fast-changing glacier on New Zealand’s South Island. It descends from high alpine snowfields toward lush temperate rainforest, creating an unusual contrast between ice and green landscape. Its dramatic valley, changing ice front, and accessibility have made it one of New Zealand’s most famous glacier locations.
keyFacts: One of New Zealand’s most accessible glaciers
Descends from the Southern Alps
Reaches unusually low elevation for a glacier
Surrounded by temperate rainforest
Part of Te Wāhipounamu World Heritage area
Known for rapid changes in length and ice conditions

name: Fox Glacier
about: Fox Glacier, also known as Te Moeka o Tuawe, is a long glacier flowing from the Southern Alps toward the rainforest-covered West Coast of New Zealand. It is famous for its steep descent, wet climate, and scenic surroundings. Like Franz Josef Glacier, it shows how ice can exist close to dense vegetation when snowfall, altitude, and glacier flow work together.
keyFacts: One of the major glaciers of New Zealand’s West Coast
Officially named Fox Glacier / Te Moeka o Tuawe
Descends toward rainforest terrain
Fed by high alpine snowfields
Known for rapid landscape changes
Located near Fox Glacier village
Popular for scenic flights and guided viewing

name: Athabasca Glacier
about: Athabasca Glacier is one of the most visited glaciers in North America and forms part of the Columbia Icefield in the Canadian Rockies. Its visible retreat has made it a powerful educational location where visitors can see how a glacier changes over time. The glacier is easily viewed from the Icefields Parkway, but walking on the ice requires proper guidance due to crevasses and unstable surfaces.
keyFacts: One of the main outlet glaciers of the Columbia Icefield
Located in the Canadian Rockies
One of the most accessible glaciers in North America
Has visibly retreated over the last century
Important for climate education and glacier tourism
Ice thickness can reach hundreds of meters in some zones

name: Columbia Glacier
about: Columbia Glacier is a large tidewater glacier in Alaska that flows from the Chugach Mountains into Prince William Sound. It is known for rapid retreat and major iceberg production. Because it ends in seawater, large icebergs can break away and drift into the surrounding fjords, making it an important glacier for observing tidewater glacier behavior.
keyFacts: Large tidewater glacier in Alaska
Flows into Prince William Sound
Known for rapid retreat since the late 20th century
Produces many icebergs through calving
Named after Columbia University
Important for studying tidewater glacier instability

name: Mendenhall Glacier
about: Mendenhall Glacier is a well-known glacier near Juneau, Alaska. It flows from the Juneau Icefield toward Mendenhall Lake and is one of the most accessible glaciers in the region. Its blue ice, nearby forest trails, visitor center, and lakefront views make it a popular place to understand glacier retreat, ice caves, and the connection between glaciers and coastal landscapes.
keyFacts: Flows from the Juneau Icefield
Ends near Mendenhall Lake
Located close to Juneau, Alaska
Known for blue ice and changing ice caves
Popular for hiking, photography, and education
Retreat has exposed new land and lake areas

name: Grey Glacier
about: Grey Glacier is one of the most iconic glaciers in Torres del Paine National Park. It flows from the Southern Patagonian Ice Field into Grey Lake, where blue icebergs often float across the water. The glacier is surrounded by mountains, wind-shaped landscapes, and dramatic Patagonian scenery, making it one of the most visually striking glacier locations in South America.
keyFacts: Part of the Southern Patagonian Ice Field
Flows into Grey Lake
Located in Torres del Paine National Park
Famous for floating blue icebergs
Popular for hiking, kayaking, and boat tours
Strong winds and cold weather shape the surrounding landscape
Important indicator of Patagonian glacier change

name: Pasterze Glacier
about: Pasterze Glacier is the largest glacier in Austria and lies below Grossglockner, the country’s highest mountain. Its long tongue once extended much farther down the valley, but today it is a visible example of rapid alpine glacier retreat. The glacier is closely connected with Austrian mountain tourism, high alpine roads, and climate observation.
keyFacts: Largest glacier in Austria
Located below Grossglockner
Part of Hohe Tauern National Park
Strongly affected by alpine warming
Popular viewing point from the Grossglockner High Alpine Road
Important symbol of glacier retreat in the Eastern Alps

name: Jostedalsbreen Glacier
about: Jostedalsbreen is the largest glacier in mainland Europe. It covers a high mountain plateau in western Norway and feeds many outlet glaciers that descend into valleys, lakes, and fjord landscapes. The glacier’s size, accessibility, and surrounding national park make it one of Norway’s most important icy landmarks.
keyFacts: Largest glacier on mainland Europe
Located in Jostedalsbreen National Park
Feeds many outlet glaciers
Surrounded by fjords, valleys, and mountain scenery
Important for tourism and glaciology in Norway
Snowfall and altitude help maintain the ice cap

name: Siachen Glacier
about: Siachen Glacier is one of the longest glaciers outside the polar regions. It lies high in the Karakoram mountains, surrounded by extreme altitude, severe cold, and rugged terrain. Its remote location and harsh conditions make it one of the most challenging glacier environments in the world.
keyFacts: One of the longest non-polar glaciers
Located in the Karakoram range
Extremely high-altitude glacier environment
Known for severe cold and difficult terrain
Fed by surrounding snowfields and mountain ice
Important part of the greater Himalayan cryosphere
Remote and rarely visited by ordinary travelers

name: Baltoro Glacier
about: Baltoro Glacier is one of the great mountain glaciers of the Karakoram. It flows through a spectacular high-altitude region surrounded by some of the world’s tallest peaks, including K2. The glacier is famous among trekkers and mountaineers because it forms a natural route toward Concordia, one of the most dramatic mountain meeting points on Earth.
keyFacts: One of the largest glaciers outside the polar regions
Located near K2 and other major Karakoram peaks
Important route for high-altitude expeditions
Flows through a dramatic mountain valley
Known for debris-covered ice and rugged terrain
Part of the complex Karakoram glacier system

name: Mer de Glace
about: Mer de Glace, meaning “Sea of Ice,” is the largest glacier in France and one of the most famous glaciers in the Alps. Located near Chamonix, it has long attracted scientists, artists, climbers, and travelers. Its retreat is clearly visible today, making it an important place for public education about glacier change.
keyFacts: Largest glacier in France
Located near Chamonix and Mont Blanc
Name means “Sea of Ice”
Historically important for alpine science and tourism
Retreat is visible from visitor viewpoints
Known for ice caves and mountain railway access
Strong symbol of climate change in the French Alps

name: Gangotri Glacier
about: Gangotri Glacier is one of the most important glaciers in the Indian Himalaya. It is closely connected with the headwaters of the Ganges river system and has cultural, spiritual, and environmental importance. The glacier flows through a high mountain valley surrounded by peaks, moraines, and rugged Himalayan terrain.
keyFacts: Major glacier in the Indian Himalaya
Source region for the Bhagirathi River
Closely connected with the Ganges river system
Important in both natural and cultural geography
Has shown long-term retreat
Surrounded by high Himalayan peaks and moraines

name: Tasman Glacier
location: Aoraki / Mount Cook National Park, New Zealand
about: Tasman Glacier is New Zealand’s longest glacier and lies in the shadow of Aoraki / Mount Cook. Its lower section is partly covered with debris, and its terminus ends near a growing proglacial lake filled with floating icebergs. The glacier shows how warming, debris cover, and lake formation can reshape a mountain glacier system.
keyFacts: Longest glacier in New Zealand
Located near Aoraki / Mount Cook
Lower glacier is partly debris-covered
Ends near Tasman Lake
Floating icebergs are often visible on the lake
Important example of lake-driven glacier retreat
Popular for viewpoints, boat tours, and alpine scenery

name: Pine Island Glacier
about: Pine Island Glacier is one of the most closely watched glaciers in Antarctica. It drains part of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet into the Amundsen Sea and is known for rapid thinning, retreat, and large iceberg calving events. Scientists monitor it carefully because changes in this glacier can influence future sea-level rise.
keyFacts: One of Antarctica’s fastest-changing glacier systems
Drains into the Amundsen Sea
Important for global sea-level research
Produces very large icebergs
Monitored by satellites and polar research missions
Connected to the vulnerable West Antarctic Ice Sheet

name: Thwaites Glacier
about: Thwaites Glacier is a massive glacier in West Antarctica and one of the most important glacier systems for modern climate research. It is often studied because its retreat could affect the stability of nearby ice and contribute to long-term sea-level rise. The glacier’s grounding zone, ice shelf, and ocean interaction make it a key focus of polar science.
keyFacts: Major glacier in West Antarctica
Drains into the Amundsen Sea
Known for rapid thinning and retreat
Closely monitored by international research teams
Important for understanding future sea-level rise
Interacts strongly with warm ocean water beneath the ice shelf
Sometimes called one of the most critical glaciers on Earth

name: Rhone Glacier
about: Rhone Glacier is an alpine glacier in Switzerland and the source region of the Rhône River. It has been observed and visited for many generations, making it historically important for glacier tourism and scientific study. Its retreat has been especially visible, and protective coverings have sometimes been used near the ice grotto area to slow seasonal melting.
keyFacts: Source region of the Rhône River
Located in the Swiss Alps
Long history of glacier observation
Popular for road-access viewing
Known for visible retreat over time
Associated with seasonal ice grotto tourism

name: Hubbard Glacier
about:
Hubbard Glacier is one of the most impressive tidewater glaciers in North America. It begins high in the mountains near the border of Alaska and Yukon, then flows toward Disenchantment Bay and Russell Fjord. Unlike many glaciers that are retreating, Hubbard has been known for periods of advance, which makes it especially interesting for glacier observation and scientific study.
Its enormous ice front rises above the water and often produces powerful calving events. When large blocks of ice break away, they fall into the bay with a deep sound and create waves across the cold water. Because the glacier reaches the ocean, it constantly interacts with tides, seawater, storms, and floating ice. This makes Hubbard Glacier a strong example of how land ice and ocean systems are connected.
keyFacts: One of the largest tidewater glaciers in North America
Flows from the Saint Elias Mountains toward the sea
Known for dramatic calving into Disenchantment Bay
Has shown periods of advance instead of constant retreat
Can temporarily block parts of Russell Fjord during major advances
Important for studying tidewater glacier behavior
Popular viewing location for cruise routes in Alaska
Its ice front can be several kilometers wide

name: Jakobshavn Glacier
about:
Jakobshavn Glacier, also known as Sermeq Kujalleq, is one of Greenland’s most famous and scientifically important glaciers. It drains ice from the Greenland Ice Sheet into Ilulissat Icefjord, where huge icebergs break away and drift toward the sea. The icebergs produced by this glacier can be enormous, sometimes towering above the water like frozen cliffs.
This glacier is especially important because it has been one of the fastest-moving and most closely studied glaciers in the world. Its speed, thinning, and calving behavior have helped scientists understand how the Greenland Ice Sheet responds to warming air and ocean conditions. The surrounding icefjord is also one of the most visually striking glacier landscapes on Earth, with floating icebergs filling the dark Arctic water.
keyFacts: One of the fastest and most active outlet glaciers in Greenland
Drains ice from the Greenland Ice Sheet into Ilulissat Icefjord
Produces very large icebergs
Located near the town of Ilulissat
Part of a UNESCO World Heritage landscape
Important for sea-level and ice-sheet research
Also known by the Greenlandic name Sermeq Kujalleq
Its ice movement has changed significantly over time
A major symbol of Arctic glacier change

name: Briksdal Glacier
about:
Briksdal Glacier is a famous outlet glacier of the larger Jostedalsbreen ice cap in Norway. It descends between steep mountain walls into the beautiful Briksdalen valley, where waterfalls, green slopes, and cold glacier streams create a dramatic Nordic landscape. The glacier has long been one of Norway’s most visited glacier locations because it is easier to reach than many remote ice formations.
The glacier is also a clear example of how outlet glaciers can change quickly. Its front has moved back and forth over time, but recent retreat has made the ice less accessible than it once was. Even so, Briksdal remains an important location for understanding how a large ice cap feeds smaller glacier arms into valleys. It is especially useful in an educational app because it connects glacier science with visible landscape change.
keyFacts: Outlet glacier of the Jostedalsbreen ice cap
Located in Briksdalen valley in western Norway
Surrounded by waterfalls, steep cliffs, and mountain scenery
One of Norway’s most famous glacier tourist sites
Shows visible retreat in the valley landscape
Fed by snow and ice from the high ice cap above
Important example of how ice caps create outlet glaciers
Often used to explain glacier retreat to visitors

name: Nigardsbreen Glacier
about:
Nigardsbreen is another well-known outlet glacier of Jostedalsbreen, the largest ice cap in mainland Europe. It flows down from the high ice plateau into a valley with a cold glacial lake at its front. The glacier is famous for its blue ice, crevasses, and strong visual contrast between white ice, dark rock, and green Norwegian valley landscapes.
This glacier is often used for guided glacier walks, but it is also a place where safety is very important. The ice front can be unstable, and falling ice or sudden changes near the glacier edge can be dangerous. For an educational app, Nigardsbreen is a strong example of how beautiful glacier landscapes require respect and careful observation. It can also help users understand glacier meltwater, proglacial lakes, and seasonal changes in ice shape.
keyFacts: Outlet glacier from Jostedalsbreen Ice Cap
Located in Jostedal, western Norway
Known for blue ice and visible crevasses
Ends near a cold glacial lake
Popular for guided glacier hiking
Ice front can be dangerous due to falling ice
Shows seasonal and long-term changes in shape
Helps explain meltwater lakes and glacier retreat
One of the most recognizable glacier arms in Norway

name: Exit Glacier
location: Kenai Fjords National Park, Alaska, USA
about:
Exit Glacier is one of the most accessible glaciers in Alaska and a major outlet of the Harding Icefield. It is located in Kenai Fjords National Park and is often visited because trails allow people to see the glacier landscape up close. Along the route, signs mark where the glacier used to reach in previous years, making it a powerful outdoor lesson about glacier retreat.
This glacier is especially useful for explaining change over time. Visitors can walk through areas that were once covered by ice and see how plants, rocks, streams, and new ground appear after a glacier pulls back. Exit Glacier is not only a natural attraction but also a clear visual timeline of retreat. In the app, it can work well as a location that connects science, hiking, climate awareness, and personal observation.
keyFacts: Outlet glacier of the Harding Icefield
Located in Kenai Fjords National Park
One of the most accessible glaciers in Alaska
Trail markers show its retreat over time
Useful for understanding landscape change after ice retreat
Surrounded by newly exposed rock, streams, and vegetation
Popular for hiking and educational visits
Strong example of visible glacier recession
Helps explain how glaciers shape and then reveal valleys

name: Malaspina Glacier
about:
Malaspina Glacier is one of the largest piedmont glaciers in the world. It spreads outward at the base of the Saint Elias Mountains, forming a huge fan-shaped ice mass near the Gulf of Alaska. Unlike narrow valley glaciers that stay between mountain walls, Malaspina flows out onto flatter land, creating a broad and dramatic ice landscape that is especially recognizable from satellite images.
The glacier is partly covered by debris, which gives it dark curved patterns and makes it look very different from clean blue-white ice glaciers. These debris lines show how the glacier has carried rock material from the mountains and spread it across the ice surface. Malaspina is an excellent example for teaching glacier types because its wide, rounded form clearly shows what a piedmont glacier is. It also connects glacier science with remote sensing, since much of its structure is best viewed from above.
keyFacts: One of the world’s largest piedmont glaciers
Spreads out in a wide fan shape near the Gulf of Alaska
Flows from the Saint Elias Mountains
Famous for its dramatic appearance in satellite images
Partly covered by rock debris and dark moraine patterns
Important example of a glacier spreading onto flatter land
Shows how glaciers transport rock material over long distances
Remote and difficult to explore on foot
Useful for explaining glacier morphology and ice movement
Covers a very large lowland ice area near the coast
